stewart_read: Randy captured the bill figure and moved on. The schema is explicit: 'capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be FLIPPED into emotional alignment-against-the-utility.' Randy's response was 'And 8 cents. Okay. Sounds good.' — pure data acknowledgment, zero emotional leverage. The flip was available: $92 is a relatively low bill, but the budget billing context (disclosed at 04:07) means Tony's actual consumption cost may be higher. The flip opportunity here was to make Tony curious about what his real cost is — not to quote a savings number, but to create a question in his mind. Reactive read holds: Randy treated the bill as a checkbox, not an anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Randy may have been holding the bill flip for the closer, keeping the setter call short as promised. However, there is no evidence of deliberate deferral — Randy simply moved to the next verify item without any acknowledgment of the bill's significance.
coaching_implication: Show Randy the schema's 'ask + acknowledge + flip' pattern. In the 1-on-1, ask him: 'What did you do with the $92 number after Tony gave it to you?' The answer will reveal whether this is a knowledge gap or a habit gap. Suggested rephrase intent (not verbatim): make Tony curious about whether his smoothed budget bill is hiding a higher real cost — that curiosity is what the closer needs walking in.

quote: I'm looking at seeing how much it's gonna cost me and what I can do with
stewart_read: Tony's opening line is an unsolicited statement of purchase intent — he wants to know cost and options. This is the emotional anchor the schema calls 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR. Depth matters more than the answer.' Randy acknowledged it with 'Yeah. Sounds good. Awesome.' and moved immediately into verify. He did not expand, did not ask what's driving the interest, did not spend the 60+ seconds the schema recommends on this item. The enthusiasm signal was present and walked past. This is a coaching gap on verify.interest_in_solar depth, not a fatal error — Tony stayed cooperative — but the closer will walk in without knowing what's motivating Tony beyond generic cost curiosity.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Randy may have judged that Tony's return-call behavior already demonstrated sufficient motivation and that dwelling on interest would feel redundant. Possible, but there is no evidence Randy made this calculation — the transition was immediate and mechanical.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Randy what he knows about WHY Tony is interested in solar. He won't have an answer beyond 'cost.' That gap is the coaching point. A single follow-up question at 00:11 — 'What's got you thinking about it now?' — would have surfaced motivation the closer could use. Note: the 'grandfather technique' of slowing down with older customers to invite them to tell their story is a suggested approach, not a validated Ion coaching move — flag for Kenny's calibration.

quote: I'm retired, so it depends on my doctor appointment schedule.
stewart_read: Tony's disclosure that he is retired and managing a dense medical appointment schedule is a scheduling constraint and a secondary vulnerability signal. Randy's response — 'Okay. Yeah. What is that looking like for you right now?' — is purely logistical. He moved directly to calendar navigation without any acknowledgment of Tony's situation. The schema classifies retirement and health-related disclosures as vulnerability categories that warrant acknowledgment before bridging to value. Randy did not acknowledge; he extracted. The miss is mild because Tony remained cooperative, but the closer will walk into an appointment with a 75-year-old managing active medical care and no context about that.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read holds. There is no evidence Randy registered this as anything other than a scheduling question. The absence of any acknowledgment phrase confirms this was not a deliberate choice to stay task-focused.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the 07:00 moment and ask Randy what he heard. If he says 'he's retired and busy,' ask what else was in that line. The coaching point is that 'doctor appointment schedule' is not just a calendar constraint — it's context about Tony's life that the closer needs and that a single empathetic line ('Sounds like you've got a full plate — we'll make sure this is worth your time') would have acknowledged without slowing the call.

quote: I am on the budget system.
stewart_read: Tony disclosed that he is on budget billing — a utility payment plan that smooths monthly charges into a fixed average rather than reflecting actual consumption. His stated bill of $92.08 is therefore a smoothed payment, not a true consumption-based cost. Randy's response was 'Okay. Okay. Awesome. Sounds good.' — no acknowledgment of what budget billing means for the design or the savings comparison. This is a material knowledge gap: the closer will walk in with $92.08 as the bill anchor and may frame savings against a number that understates Tony's actual energy cost. The gap affects both the design accuracy and the savings pitch credibility.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Randy may have known that the kWh readback (which followed immediately) would give the design team the actual consumption data regardless of the billing format, making the dollar figure less critical. Partially valid — the kWh data does correct for budget billing at the design level. However, the closer still needs to know the bill figure is smoothed when framing the savings conversation, and Randy gave no indication he flagged this.
coaching_implication: Add budget billing to Randy's knowledge base: budget billing means the monthly dollar figure is an average, not a real-time cost signal. The kWh data Randy collected is the right anchor for the design, but the closer needs to know the $92.08 is a smoothed number. This belongs in the handoff note. In the 1-on-1, ask Randy: 'If Tony's on budget billing and his actual summer usage is 900 kWh, what does that do to the $92 number?' If he doesn't know, that's the coaching gap.

quote: Who are you folks based out of?
stewart_read: Tony's question about company location is a legitimacy probe — he wants to know if this is a local or distant operation. Randy answered directly: 'We're actually based out of Utah. That's where I'm calling you from, but we are nationwide and we're local here in Virginia.' The answer is honest and includes the local-presence reassurance that matters to the customer. No schema protocol covers geographic-origin questions specifically (protocols.how_did_you_get_my_number covers data-sourcing questions, not location questions — that citation does not apply here). Randy handled this correctly with no coaching gap. Surfaced only because the question is a legitimacy signal worth the manager noting: Tony was doing his own due diligence mid-call.
coaching_implication: No action needed on this moment. Note for the manager: Tony's mid-call legitimacy probe is consistent with a 75-year-old customer being appropriately cautious. Randy's transparent answer was the right move. If anything, this moment could be used as a positive example in team training — direct, honest, local-presence reassurance without defensiveness.

quote: Thanks for reading those out to me, Tommy. I'll be able to get this sent over to our design team to have that design put together for you.
stewart_read: Randy's transition from bill collection to appointment scheduling is clean and scope-appropriate. He correctly positions the design as something the specialist team produces — not something he explains — and uses it as the natural bridge to booking. This is the universal escape hatch in action: 'I'm scheduling, they're solving.' The micro-trade here is Randy converting the kWh readback effort (which Tony invested real time in) into forward momentum toward the appointment. Tony's cooperation on the readback created a small commitment that Randy correctly leveraged into the scheduling ask.
coaching_implication: This is a positive moment worth naming in the 1-on-1. Randy held setter scope cleanly at the transition point — he did not explain what the design would show, did not quote savings, did not scope-creep into closer territory. Use this as the anchor for the bill-flip coaching: 'You held scope perfectly here. The one thing that was available before this transition was a single emotional line about what the design is going to reveal for Tony specifically. That's not scope creep — that's curiosity creation.'

stewart_read: Tony writing down the appointment details is a positive commitment signal. However, Randy's button-up is structurally incomplete against the schema's required_elements. Missing: (1) one_hour_block_stated — Randy never told Tony the appointment is an hour; (2) no_driving_at_computer — Randy never asked Tony to be at a computer and not driving; (3) is_that_fair_handshake — Randy closed with 'we'll see you next Wednesday' rather than a verbal handshake. The phone_appointment_specified element is also ambiguous — Randy said 'I'll send you a confirmation text' but never explicitly confirmed this is a phone call, not an in-person visit. Tony's 'I'm writing this down' suggests he's treating this as a real commitment, which is good — but the structural anchors that protect show rate were not set.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Randy may have judged that Tony's cooperative demeanor made the full button-up protocol unnecessary. Reactive read holds — there is no evidence of deliberate compression, and the missing elements (computer, no-driving, is-that-fair) are show-rate predictors the schema marks as required.
coaching_implication: Walk Randy through the button_up required_elements checklist against this call. The missing items are not rapport failures — Tony is warm — but they are structural gaps that increase no-show risk with a 75-year-old managing a busy medical schedule. Specifically: (1) confirm it's a phone call, not a visit; (2) ask Tony to be at a computer to review the design; (3) close with 'Is that fair?' The handshake matters most with older customers who may not distinguish between 'I wrote it down' and 'I committed to show up.'

quote: I'm looking at seeing how much it's gonna cost me and what I can do with
stewart_read: Verify completion assessment for this call: address/homeowner confirmed (00:33–00:49) ✓; title/co-owners confirmed (01:07–01:13) ✓; residence length captured (00:51–00:58) ✓; interest_in_solar — stated but not expanded (00:11) partial; utility company confirmed (03:07–03:12) ✓; average bill captured (03:21) ✓; roof type captured (01:27–01:35) ✓; roof age — NOT captured ✗; prior designs — NOT captured ✗. Estimated verify completion rate: 6/8 required items = 75%, meeting the schema's 6-of-8 threshold. The two misses are roof age (hard_dq_if: roof_age_over_15_no_replacement_planned) and prior designs. Roof age is low-risk given Tony's 3-year tenure in what he described as a 'brand new home' — a new construction home almost certainly has a roof under 5 years old. Prior designs miss is a minor competitive intelligence gap.
coaching_implication: Flag roof age as a habit gap even though the risk is low here. Randy should ask it on every call — the 3-year tenure inference is not a substitute for the explicit question. Prior designs is a softer miss: Tony said this is his first time looking at solar (03:39), which partially covers the intent of the prior_designs item, but the explicit question was never asked. In the 1-on-1, use this call to walk Randy through the verify checklist and identify which items he's consistently skipping across calls.
